captions.text: I don't really engage with hype for much the same  reason I don't lie down under a vibrating drainage
outlet with my eyes and mouth pinned open, so  I was a little thrown when Editor Nick gave me
a review code for a game I'd never heard of in my  life and told me it was the most hotly anticipated
new action-adventure of the year so far. Of  course, that's no immediate indicator of quality;
I mean, an enormous, copious piss would be hotly  anticipated if the bus driver was being an asshole
about stopping for bathroom breaks for six hours,  but it's not the sort of thing I'd try to impress
my mates with. And a small amount of investigation  revealed that Crimson Desert is one of those open
world action-adventures that come along now and  again, usually to sucker in Kickstarter backers,
that purport to do EVERYTHING. So it's got Soulsy  combat and puzzles and base management and faction
reputations and crafting and physics manipulation,  and you can go anywhere with Breath of the Wild
climbing mechanics, and try to imagine as I'm  reading this a placid half-smile on my face as
inside my brain a fucking gorilla with a siren  light strapped to their head is going to town
on a gigantic fuck-off alarm bell with a  clapper with a big red flag on the end.
We've been here before. Games that try to be about  everything, on the rare occasions they escape the
rigors of hype hell, always turn out to be games  about nothing; freakish, stitched-together,
inferior versions of nine other games with better  focus that we might as well play instead if we're
in the mood for that. But I always give the  benefit of the doubt if your name's not David
Cage, so I gave Crimson Desert a shot. And  it left a good initial first impression by,
in defiance of expectations, being about  something. Specifically, it's about an
almost aggressively generic medieval fantasy  world in which our protagonist is a big, hairy,
angry Scotsman named—checks notes—Cliff. Cliff. As  in Richard. As in Bleszinski. That's our fantasy
protagonist name, is it? Cliff is the name of the  asshole in the friend group who insists on doing
two karaoke songs in a row, not— "Wait, Yahtzee!  Look! Kliff is spelled with a K!" Oh, so it is.
Sorry, Crimson Desert. Never mind; as you were.  Klifford the Big Red Scotsman is a member of a
group called the Greymanes who do... something.  They're possibly some kind of mercenary group
that travels the land doing good deeds because,  I dunno, maybe they've got nonprofit tax status?
Well, whatever the fuck they do, it apparently  put a bug right up the arse of another mercenary
group of unclear purpose called the Black Bears  who kick the shit out of the Greymanes in the
prologue and leave Kliff for dead. Or possibly  outright kill him; it's unclear, but Kliff comes
back to life for equally unclear reasons, and  then a weird thing happens: the distinct hook and
villain established by that prologue mysteriously  vanish. Kliff wakes up in a peasant village very
conspicuously NOT under threat by the Black Bears,  where some random bloke goes, "Hey, good to see
you're up and about. Wanna join me for a walk into  town?" And then the critical path objective list
basically has us putter around, doing light  favors for random people: we tip a beggar;
we clean a chimney; we get a cat down from a  roof and generally channel the Pleasantville
fire department, after which a goddess appears to  us and goes, "Ah, you have proved you are a man
of humility and goodness. Now I will introduce you  to the god of the timelines, who will provide you
with your quest and great godlike power." "Ooh,  that should come in handy when I'm enacting bloody
revenge upon those Black Bear motherfuckers!"  Yeah, maybe don't lead with that, Kliff. Anyway,
God tells us that our world is in imbalance and we  need to sort it out by killing all the evil dudes
in it or something, which is certainly easier than  coming up with an actual plot. Am I right, devs?
So the story element is like a blind bait shop  assistant in that it fails to find a hook. But
I did admire the way the game goes out of its  way to avoid the BioWare face effect during
conversations; instead of maintaining fixed  eye contact, the characters just wander a few
steps away and then stop every now and again like  they're trying to escape an oblivious talkative
person at a party. Other good points: the world is  certainly very large and impressive with much to
explore and discover, which is much harder to  appreciate when you're on a fucking deadline,
but it's not like you people ever sympathize,  and the game functions surprisingly well for
all its multiple competing systems; the traversal  and the physics never seem to bug out, and while
I wouldn't bother engaging with the inevitable  stealth option 'cause enemies spot you if you so
much as think about loud things in their presence,  the combat has a surprisingly decent feel:
Soulslike-y but often against multiple targets in  a nicely realized Batman Arkham sort of way. The
problem—and it's a big one that undermines  everything else—is that the controls are
horribly clunky. Which is a direct consequence  of the "we do everything" remit because it means
at any moment, we need to have access to the  controls of nine different gameplay systems.
I kept accidentally pressing the analog stick  in and bringing out my Garry's Mod telekinesis
power in the middle of a fight, and the enemy  were singularly unimpressed by my ability to
twiddle their nipples with no hands. And because  pressing in the stick was reserved for that, we
sprint by tapping A, as in that godawful Euphoria  engine style that was annoying enough while trying
to find cover in Grand Theft Auto; when you're  up against some of the relentless boss fights
in this game who keep spamming their bullshit  attacks, it's like trying to escape from a swarm
of angry hornets in a temperamental forklift.  You've got so many moves and powers available,
you need a fucking cheat sheet in your lap  to remember all the button combinations.
Hold both shoulder buttons to use your Shadow  of the Colossus "reflect the light from your
sword to reveal secrets" thing, but it only  works if you've put your sword away first,
so find the button that does that, but of  course using it makes you get your sword out,
so you have to put it away again to double  check. And if you've gotten your pickaxe
out to mine ore by selecting it twice and then  very carefully aiming at the ore because hey,
maybe you intended to uselessly hit the  bit of wall next to the ore, we don't know,
and then you get ambushed on the way home and  forgot you haven't unequipped your pickaxe and
get humiliatingly raked across the ribcage while  demonstrating your slow-motion overarm swing.
Perhaps the complex mechanics wouldn't be so bad  if the game wasn't so bad at explaining them,
something which caused multiple roadblocks  on the critical path when yet another new
mechanic got badly tutorialized. "Assign  a quest to an NPC follower to continue."
"How? I'm not seeing that in either of the  two unrelated menus headed 'quests.' Oh,
I see. I was supposed to press Y on a very  specific part of the map and then present
two forms of ID at the door." But there's  plenty to complain about that's unrelated
to the mechanical glut. I don't feel like I'm  getting stronger because it takes an hour-long
sightseeing tour of popular mining hotspots to  gather the materials to up my sword damage by,
like, two if I'm lucky. I hate how you can't  skip cutscenes, only play them at double speed,
which still takes too long but now you have to  watch the characters frantically bob around like
farmyard hens. I finally lost patience and  rage quit at a specific boss fight—relying
again on poorly explained mechanics—that  they forgot to put an autosave in front of,
so after every attempt, I had to farmyard hen bob  my way through a lengthy cutscene AND complete a
tedious busywork open quotes "physics puzzle"  in which I just had to Garry's Mod four cubes
into the cube-shaped holes right next to them.  No. Enough. There's only one scenario in which I
tolerate this much putting things in holes, and  you don't look much like a peanut butter cup.
